pytorch_task_v1.prompt_instructions: Implement this ML task as a SINGLE self-contained Python file (task.py). Include all required functions AND a complete `if __name__ == '__main__':` block that trains the model, evaluates on a validation split, prints standard metrics (MSE, R2, parameter accuracy where applicable), and asserts quality thresholds so the script exits non-zero on failure. Do NOT create separate test files or README. The script itself is the test.
evaluation_rules: The `evaluate()` function MUST compute and return standard metrics: MSE, R2 score, and any task-specific metrics on the VALIDATION data split.

The `if __name__ == '__main__':` block MUST: (1) train the model, (2) evaluate on BOTH train and validation splits, (3) print all metrics clearly, (4) assert quality thresholds (e.g., R2 > 0.9, MSE < threshold), (5) exit 0 on success or non-zero on failure.
Do NOT use a separate test file. All evaluation logic goes in main().

tasks.description: Implement Univariate Linear Regression using ONLY PyTorch tensors. Do NOT use torch.nn, torch.optim, or autograd. Write everything in a single task.py file with a complete main() that trains, evaluates, and validates.

requirements.math: Docstring must contain h_theta(x) = theta_0 + theta_1*x and the MSE cost function formula.
requirements.data: Synthetic: y = 2x + 3 + noise. Split into 80% train / 20% validation.
requirements.implementation: Manual GD update: theta = theta - lr * grad (compute grad by hand). No torch.nn, no torch.optim, no autograd.
requirements.evaluation: The evaluate() function must compute MSE, R2 score, and parameter accuracy (how close theta_0 is to 3.0 and theta_1 is to 2.0) on the VALIDATION split.
requirements.main_block: The if __name__ == '__main__' block must: train for enough epochs, print train/val MSE, R2, learned parameters vs true, and assert R2 > 0.9 on validation and parameter error < 1.0.
requirements.output: Return dict with loss_history, val_loss_history, final metrics.

tasks.description: Implement Multivariate Linear Regression using torch.autograd. Visualize training.

requirements.data: Synthetic multivariate: X shape [100, 5].
requirements.implementation: Use loss.backward(); update in torch.no_grad(); implement train().
requirements.validation: Loss decreases monotonically after warmup (allow small noise).

tasks.description: Polynomial regression with L2 regularization using torch.nn + torch.optim; device agnostic.

requirements.data: Nonlinear: y = x^2 + noise; manual poly features.
requirements.implementation: torch.nn.Linear; SGD(momentum) OR weight_decay; cuda/cpu safe.
requirements.validation: Compare train vs val loss; avoid severe overfit.

tasks.description: EDA + preprocessing + PyTorch vs Sklearn on real dataset with standardized JSON output.

requirements.data: Use sklearn.datasets.fetch_california_housing; train/test split.
requirements.eda: Correlation matrix + target distribution; save plots.
requirements.preprocessing: Manual StandardScaler for PyTorch; sklearn scaler for sklearn.
requirements.api: PyTorch model wrapped with fit()/predict() sklearn-style.
requirements.comparison: Report R2 and RMSE; assert PyTorch within 5% of sklearn.
requirements.output: Standard outputs schema with metrics comparison.

tasks.algorithm: Linear Regression (Diabetes Dataset + Early Stopping Comparison)
tasks.description: Use sklearn diabetes dataset; compare PyTorch vs Sklearn; implement and compare early stopping vs no early stopping.

requirements.eda: Correlation matrix + target distribution (disease_progression); save plots.
requirements.preprocessing: Manual StandardScaler for PyTorch; sklearn scaler for sklearn.
requirements.early_stopping: Implement early stopping with patience=20 on validation loss; compare with no early stopping (200 epochs).
requirements.training: Train two models: (1) no early stopping (200 epochs), (2) with early stopping (patience=20); track train/val loss for both.
requirements.api: PyTorch model wrapped with fit()/predict() sklearn-style; support early_stopping parameter.
requirements.comparison: Report metrics for both approaches; compare epochs trained, final R2/RMSE, and assess early stopping effectiveness.
requirements.visualization: Save training history plots for both approaches; overlay comparison; correlation matrix; target distribution.
requirements.output: Metrics JSON comparing both early stopping approaches; training history JSON for both; standard comparison with sklearn.

tasks.algorithm: Cubic Polynomial Regression (Ridge + SGD + Feature Scaling)
tasks.description: Cubic polynomial regression with L2 regularization, feature scaling to prevent gradient explosion, and GPU support.

requirements.math: Cubic model: y = β₀ + β₁x + β₂x² + β₃x³ + noise. Include L2 regularization formula.
requirements.data: Synthetic cubic data: y = 1.0 + 1.5x + 1.0x² + 0.5x³ + noise. Split into 80% train / 20% validation.
requirements.implementation: Linear layer over 4 features (bias + 3 polynomial terms).
requirements.validation: Evaluate on both train and test splits. Report MSE and R² score.

tasks.algorithm: Polynomial Linear Regression (Adam + ReduceLROnPlateau + BigQuery Natality)
tasks.description: Predict infant birth weight from maternal and pregnancy features loaded from the BigQuery public natality dataset (137 M rows). Polynomial expansion of the dominant predictor (gestation_weeks) plus interaction terms allows a single linear layer to approximate the nonlinear fetal growth curve.
tasks.how_data_is_loaded_from_bigquery: A google-cloud-bigquery Python client is created with the caller's GCP project ID. Authentication is resolved automatically from Application Default Credentials (gcloud auth application-default login). The SQL query uses TABLESAMPLE SYSTEM(2 PERCENT) to randomly sample ~2.7 M rows at the BigQuery storage layer before any filtering — avoiding a full 137 M-row table scan. A WHERE clause then applies NULL checks and range filters (gestation 20–45 weeks, weight 1–15 lbs, mother age 14–50), and LIMIT 150000 caps the result. client.query(sql).to_dataframe() streams the result into a local pandas DataFrame via the BigQuery Storage Read API (fast columnar path). Boolean columns (cigarette_use, alcohol_use) are cast to INT64 directly in SQL so they arrive as 0/1 integers without any Python post-processing.
tasks.going_beyond: This task goes beyond the reference examples in three ways: (1) It uses a new, large-scale real-world dataset — the public natality table with 137 M rows of US birth records — rather than a synthetic or toy dataset. (2) It explores a new BigQuery feature: TABLESAMPLE SYSTEM, a native block-level random sampler that operates at the storage layer and is far more efficient than ORDER BY RAND(). (3) It introduces polynomial feature engineering (gestation_weeks², gestation_weeks³) and cross-feature interaction terms (gestation × weight_gain, mother_age × cigarette_use) in Python post-load, showing the full data-to-model pipeline spanning both BigQuery SQL and PyTorch.

requirements.math: Polynomial linear model: weight = W·φ(x) + b, where φ(x) = [mother_age, gestation_weeks, gestation_weeks², gestation_weeks³, weight_gain_pounds, plurality, ever_born, cigarette_use, alcohol_use, gestation×weight_gain, gestation×plurality, mother_age×cigarette_use]. Features standardized with StandardScaler before training.
requirements.data: Loaded via google-cloud-bigquery from `bigquery-public-data.samples.natality` using TABLESAMPLE SYSTEM(2 PERCENT) + WHERE + LIMIT 150000. Split 70% train / 15% val / 15% test.
requirements.implementation: nn.Linear(12 → 1) with no activation. MSELoss, Adam optimiser (weight_decay=1e-4), ReduceLROnPlateau scheduler (patience=8, factor=0.5). Best val checkpoint restored after training.
requirements.validation: Evaluate on train, val, and test splits. Report MSE, RMSE, R², and MAE. Save metrics.json and training_history.json. Assert val R² ≥ 0.35 and val MSE ≤ 1.8.

tasks.description: Binary logistic regression with manual sigmoid and manual gradients (no autograd).

requirements.data: Two Gaussian clusters; standardized features.
requirements.implementation: Manual gradient for weights + bias; GD loop.
requirements.validation: Assert accuracy > 0.90.

requirements.implementation: nn.Module; Adam; CrossEntropyLoss.

tasks.description: Multiclass softmax regression on Iris dataset; compare three optimizers (Adam, SGD, RMSProp); measure training time and evaluation metrics for each optimizer.

requirements.dataset: Iris dataset from sklearn (150 samples, 4 features, 3 classes).
requirements.implementation: nn.Module; CrossEntropyLoss; compare Adam, SGD, RMSProp optimizers.
requirements.training: 100 epochs for each optimizer; measure and report total training time.
requirements.evaluation: Report validation accuracy and F1 macro for each optimizer. Identify optimizer with fastest convergence (lowest training time) and best performance (highest validation accuracy).
requirements.validation: Train Accuracy > 0.85, Val Accuracy > 0.70, Val F1 Macro > 0.85, Train Loss < 1.0, Accuracy gap < 0.15 (applied to all optimizers).
requirements.output: Save training_history.json (all optimizer loss curves), metrics.json (all optimizer metrics), times.json (training times for each optimizer). Report comparison table and identify fastest optimizer for convergence.

tasks.description: Imbalanced binary classification with weighted loss + early stopping; manual metrics.

requirements.data: Imbalanced synthetic: 95/5.
requirements.implementation: Weighted CE (manual or by class weights), early stopping on val F1.
requirements.metrics: Manual confusion matrix, precision/recall/F1; verify vs sklearn.
requirements.validation: Recall improved vs unweighted baseline.

tasks.description: Multiclass (5 classes) imbalanced classification on KDD Cup 99 network intrusion detection dataset comparing Weighted CrossEntropyLoss vs Focal Loss for handling severe class imbalance.

requirements.data: KDD Cup 99 Network Intrusion Detection: Top 5 most common attack classes with severe real-world class imbalance (normal traffic dominant). Categorical features encoded via hashing; standardized features.
requirements.implementation: Train two models: (1) Weighted CrossEntropyLoss, (2) Focal Loss (gamma=2.0). Both with early stopping on weighted F1.
requirements.loss_functions: Implement FocalLoss as: FL(p_t) = -(1-p_t)^gamma * log(p_t) with alpha weighting for class imbalance.
requirements.metrics: For each loss function, compute accuracy, precision/recall/F1 (macro and weighted), MSE, and R2 score on validation split.
requirements.comparison: Compare both loss functions; save separate metrics JSON files for each loss. Mention which loss performs better in metrics files and print statements.
requirements.visualization: Plot training loss and validation F1 for both loss functions side-by-side.
requirements.evaluation: evaluate() must return: accuracy, precision_macro, recall_macro, f1_macro, precision_weighted, recall_weighted, weighted_f1, mse, r2.
requirements.validation: Both models must achieve accuracy > 0.50, weighted_f1 > 0.30, and MSE < 0.50 on validation split.

tasks.description: Probability calibration (Platt/Isotonic via sklearn for reference) + threshold optimization in PyTorch.

requirements.data: Real dataset: sklearn breast cancer or similar.
requirements.implementation: Compute ECE (Expected Calibration Error) manually; optimize threshold for F1.
requirements.validation: ECE decreases after calibration step (compare before/after).

tasks.description: Binary classification to identify Gentoo penguins by physical measurements loaded from the BigQuery public ml_datasets.penguins table. The binary target label is derived entirely via a CASE WHEN expression inside the SQL query, demonstrating BigQuery-side feature engineering before any data reaches Python.
tasks.how_data_is_loaded_from_bigquery: A google-cloud-bigquery Python client is created with the caller's GCP project ID, authenticating via Application Default Credentials. The SQL query selects four physical measurement columns with explicit CAST(... AS FLOAT64) for type safety, and derives the binary target label is_gentoo = CASE WHEN species LIKE '%Gentoo%' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END entirely inside BigQuery — so no label encoding is needed in Python. A WHERE clause filters out any rows where any measurement column is NULL. client.query(sql).to_dataframe() streams the result into a local pandas DataFrame. The resulting DataFrame is immediately split and standardized with StandardScaler before conversion to PyTorch TensorDatasets.
tasks.going_beyond: This task goes beyond the reference examples in two ways: (1) It applies a different algorithm — logistic regression for binary classification — contrasting directly with the linear regression task above. (2) It uses a new BigQuery feature engineering pattern: the binary target label is computed via a CASE WHEN expression directly in the SQL query, meaning BigQuery performs the label encoding before any data leaves the cloud. This is a practical pattern for production ML pipelines where label logic should live with the data, not in application code.

requirements.math: Logistic model: P(Gentoo) = σ(β₀ + β₁·culmen_length + β₂·culmen_depth + β₃·flipper_length + β₄·body_mass). σ is the sigmoid function. Binary Cross-Entropy loss.
requirements.data: Loaded via google-cloud-bigquery from `bigquery-public-data.ml_datasets.penguins`. Binary label is_gentoo derived in SQL via CASE WHEN. NULL rows filtered in WHERE clause. Stratified split 70% train / 15% val / 15% test.
requirements.implementation: nn.Linear(4 → 1) + sigmoid activation. BCELoss, SGD with momentum=0.9, CosineAnnealingLR scheduler over 150 epochs.
requirements.visualization: Save logreg_bq_penguins_roc_curve.png (ROC + training history) and logreg_bq_penguins_confusion.png (confusion matrix heatmap)
requirements.validation: Evaluate on train, val, and test splits. Report accuracy, F1, ROC-AUC, MSE (Brier), and BCE loss. Save metrics.json and training_history.json. Assert val accuracy ≥ 0.88 and val ROC-AUC ≥ 0.92.

tasks.description: Same MLP stack as lvl2 (dropout, BatchNorm) on sklearn Covertype tabular data; AdamW and class-weighted cross-entropy for imbalance.
tasks.going_beyond: Uses the real UCI Covertype benchmark (fetch_covtype: 54 features, 7 classes) instead of synthetic data. Adds stratified subsampling for practical runtimes, train-only StandardScaler, AdamW with weight decay, and inverse-frequency loss weights fit on the training split only to address class imbalance.

requirements.data: sklearn.datasets.fetch_covtype; map targets from 1–7 to 0–6 for CrossEntropyLoss.
requirements.preprocessing: Optional stratified subsample (default max 100k rows); stratified 80/20 train–val split; StandardScaler fit on training data only.
requirements.implementation: nn.Module MLP with configurable dropout and BatchNorm1d; AdamW optimizer; CrossEntropyLoss with per-class weights from training label counts.
requirements.validation: main() prints train and validation metrics; asserts train accuracy > 0.55, val accuracy > 0.5, val F1 (weighted) > 0.45, mild overfit gap (val acc ≥ train acc − 0.15), and training loss decreases; sys.exit(1) if checks fail.

tasks.description: Grid or random search over depth/width/lr/weight_decay on sklearn RCV1 multilabel topic labels; best config by validation micro-F1; AdamW and BCEWithLogitsLoss.
tasks.going_beyond: Uses sklearn.datasets.fetch_rcv1 (sparse TF-IDF features and multilabel indicators) instead of synthetic regression data. Applies TruncatedSVD and StandardScaler fit on training data only to obtain dense inputs for the MLP. Multilabel targets use BCEWithLogitsLoss; optimization uses AdamW (distinct from plain Adam). Leaderboard rows include val_mse, val_r2, val_mae, val_micro_f1, and val_macro_f1.

requirements.data: sklearn.datasets.fetch_rcv1 with subset='train'; optional subsample (default max 6000 documents) with fixed random_state for reproducibility; stratified-style random train/val/test split via train_test_split.
requirements.preprocessing: TruncatedSVD (default 256 components, capped by sample/feature count) fit on training features only; StandardScaler on SVD output fit on train only.
requirements.implementation: nn.Module MLP with per-label logits; BCEWithLogitsLoss; AdamW; ReduceLROnPlateau on validation loss; early stopping. hyperparameter_search(train_loader, val_loader, input_dim, output_dim, ...) must use the same loaders as final training so splits match.
requirements.output: metrics.json includes sweep leaderboard under metrics['sweep'], best_config, and train/validation/test metric dicts (mse, r2, mae, micro_f1, macro_f1).
requirements.evaluation: evaluate() returns MSE, R2, and MAE computed on sigmoid probabilities vs binary targets (flattened for R2), plus micro_f1 and macro_f1 at threshold 0.5 on the given loader. Use a numerically stable sigmoid for NumPy (e.g. branch on sign of logits) so large |logits| do not overflow exp.
requirements.validation: main() trains, evaluates on train and validation (and test), prints all metrics, asserts val micro_f1 >= 0.12 and val mse <= 0.45, and asserts the sweep-selected config’s final val micro-F1 is at least the baseline (depth=2, width=32) val micro-F1. After save_artifacts, reload model.pt with config.json, re-run evaluate() on val and test, assert metrics match the in-memory results and metrics.json within tolerance, and re-assert baseline/micro-F1/MSE gates on the reloaded weights; sys.exit(1) on failure.

tasks.description: Small CNN on sklearn 8x8 digits; compare Adam, SGD+Nesterov, and AdamW with the same epoch budget and ReduceLROnPlateau; pick best by validation accuracy.
tasks.going_beyond: Uses sklearn.datasets.load_digits (see https://scikit-learn.org/stable/modules/generated/sklearn.datasets.load_digits.html) with stratified train/val/test split and pixel scaling to [0,1]. Contrasts three optimizers (Adam vs SGD+momentum+Nesterov vs AdamW with stronger decoupled weight decay) rather than only swapping data. evaluate() reports classification loss and accuracy plus MSE and R2 between one-hot labels and softmax probabilities for pytorch_task_v1 alignment.

requirements.data: sklearn.datasets.load_digits; stratified 70/15/15 train/val/test; features reshaped to Nx1x8x8 and divided by 16.0.
requirements.architecture: Compact CNN: two conv+ReLU+maxpool blocks, dropout on the fully connected head, 10-way logits.
requirements.implementation: For each optimizer, fresh model with seed 42, identical epochs and ReduceLROnPlateau on validation loss; keep weights from the run with highest final validation accuracy. Required protocol functions: get_task_metadata, set_seed, get_device, make_dataloaders, build_model, train, evaluate, predict, save_artifacts.
requirements.evaluation: evaluate() returns loss, accuracy, mse, r2 on the given loader; main() prints train/val/test metrics for the selected best model.
requirements.visualization: Confusion matrix on validation for the best model; optimizer_comparison_val_acc.png overlaying validation accuracy curves.
requirements.output: metrics.json includes optimizer_comparison (per-optimizer val accuracy, MSE, R2, hyperparameters), best_optimizer, optimizer_val_accuracies histories, and best-model train/val/test metrics; model.pth state dict.
requirements.validation: main() asserts val accuracy >= 0.92, every optimizer run reaches val accuracy >= 0.75, and the saved best model is at least as good as the standalone Adam run on validation.

tasks.description: Fine-tune pretrained ResNet on CIFAR10 or a small custom dataset; freeze/unfreeze stages.

requirements.validation: Transfer learning beats training-from-scratch baseline.

tasks.algorithm: Transfer Learning (ResNet18 on Olivetti Faces + Optimizer Comparison)
tasks.description: Fine-tune pretrained ResNet18 on sklearn Olivetti Faces (400 images, 40 classes); compare Adam vs SGD with Nesterov; implement grayscale-to-RGB expansion for transfer learning compatibility.
tasks.going_beyond: Uses the sklearn Olivetti faces dataset, which requires handling grayscale images for a model (ResNet) pretrained on RGB ImageNet. It compares two different optimization strategies — Adam (adaptive) vs SGD with Nesterov Momentum and Weight Decay (classic) — to evaluate which converges better on a very small, high-dimensional dataset (10 samples per class). It also implements a staged fine-tuning approach: first training the head, then unfreezing and fine-tuning the entire network.

requirements.data: sklearn.datasets.fetch_olivetti_faces; 400 images of 64x64 pixel grayscale faces; stratified 80/20 train/val split (since dataset is very small, we skip a separate test set or use val as test).
requirements.preprocessing: Convert grayscale [1, 64, 64] to RGB [3, 64, 64] by repeating channels; Normalize with ImageNet mean/std.
requirements.implementation: Compare two optimizers: (1) Adam (lr=1e-3), (2) SGD with Nesterov Momentum (lr=1e-3, momentum=0.9, nesterov=True) and Weight Decay (1e-4). Use a two-stage training strategy: 10 epochs with a frozen base (head only), followed by 10 epochs unfrozen (fine-tuning).
requirements.evaluation: evaluate() returns accuracy, loss, MSE, and R2 (between probabilities and one-hot labels). Report metrics for both optimization methods.
requirements.visualization: Save 'optimizer_comparison.png' showing validation accuracy curves; Save 'olivetti_predictions.png' showing a grid of test images with predictions.
requirements.validation: Final validation accuracy for the best model must be >= 0.85; Both optimizers must reach >= 0.50 val accuracy. Compare and print which optimizer performed better.
